name: Vallabhacharya
dates_notes: Mughal-era court records and sectarian chronicles align.

dates_evidence.description: Mughal-period court records of his life and patronage.

core_thesis: Vallabha's *śuddhādvaita* — "pure non-dualism" — holds that Brahman, identified with Kṛṣṇa, is absolutely non-dual and the sole reality, and that the world and the *jīvas* are equally real precisely as Brahman's own *aṃśas*. Crucially, this is non-dualism *without* *māyā*: the world is not illusory appearance but a genuine *avirbhāva–tirobhāva* — manifestation and concealment — of Brahman's own *sat-cit-ānanda* qualities, sometimes labelled *avikṛta-pariṇāma-vāda* (transformation-without-mutation). *Jīva* manifests *sat* and *cit* with *ānanda* concealed; *jagat* manifests *sat* with *cit* and *ānanda* concealed. The world's reality is essential to the position and is the principal contrast with Śaṅkara's Advaita, with which *śuddhādvaita* should not be conflated. Liberation is not realisation of acosmic identity but the awakening of *bhakti* and entry into Bhagavān's *līlā*. The lineage's distinctive devotional path is *Puṣṭi-Mārga* — the path of grace — in which liberation is bestowed by Kṛṣṇa's free *anugraha* on the *bhakta* he selects, and *seva* (Deity-service) is the operative practice.

engaged_works.title: Anu-Bhashya
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored for the portion Vallabha himself completed; the third-pāda-onward continuation was by his son Viṭṭhalanātha.
engaged_works.summary: The *Aṇu-Bhāṣya* is Vallabha's Brahma-Sūtra commentary and the school-defining bhāṣya of *śuddhādvaita*. "Aṇu" ("small") refers both to its terse glossing style and to its self-presentation alongside the lost or projected *Bṛhad-Bhāṣya*. Vallabha completed roughly the first two and a half *adhyāyas* before his death; the remainder was supplied by his son Viṭṭhalanātha. Doctrinally the bhāṣya construes the *sūtras* throughout as licensing *avikṛta-pariṇāma-vāda*: Brahman is the substantial cause of the world by genuine but unmodifying transformation, the *jīva* is *aṃśa* with *ānanda* concealed, *jagat* is *aṃśa* with *cit* and *ānanda* concealed, and *māyā-vāda* is rejected. Polemically the work targets both Śaṅkara — for treating the world as *mithyā* and Brahman as inactive — and Rāmānuja — for the *śarīra-śarīrin* qualification that Vallabha takes as superfluous once *avikṛta-pariṇāma* is admitted. It is the canonical Puṣṭimārga doctrinal reference.

engaged_works.title: Tattvartha-Dipa-Nibandha
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored systematic exposition of Śuddhādvaita.
engaged_works.summary: The *Tattvārtha-Dīpa-Nibandha* is Vallabha's three-part systematic exposition of *śuddhādvaita*, organised as commentary-cum-exposition on three *prasthāna* texts: *Śāstrārtha-Prakaraṇa* (which sets out the *prasthāna-traya* method and Vallabha's interpretive principles), *Sarva-Nirṇaya-Prakaraṇa* (which fixes the school's settled doctrinal positions across *Brahman*, *jīva*, *jagat*, *bandha*, *mokṣa*, and *bhakti*), and *Bhāgavata-Artha-Prakaraṇa* (which establishes the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* as the *pramāṇa-śiromaṇi* and reads its central themes through *śuddhādvaita*). The *Nibandha* is the school's most accessible synoptic statement, free from the *sūtra*-form constraints of the *Aṇu-Bhāṣya* and free from the verse-by-verse pacing of the *Subodhinī*. It establishes *avikṛta-pariṇāma-vāda* as the school's signature ontological thesis and *Puṣṭi-Mārga* as its operative soteriology, and provides the polemical framing against Advaita and Viśiṣṭādvaita that all later Puṣṭimārga scholasticism inherits.

engaged_works.title: Subodhini on Bhagavata
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored Bhāgavata commentary; Vallabha completed a portion, with Viṭṭhalanātha continuing.
engaged_works.summary: The *Subodhinī* is Vallabha's verse-by-verse commentary on the *Bhāgavata-Purāṇa* and is the most extensive of his works. He reads the *Bhāgavata* as the *naijagama-bhāṣya* — the natural commentary on the *Brahma-Sūtras* and Upaniṣads — and so as the supreme *pramāṇa* for *śuddhādvaita*. Vallabha completed substantial portions (notably on books one through three and most of the tenth book) before his death; Viṭṭhalanātha continued the work. The commentary is doctrinally precise — Brahman as Kṛṣṇa with all *guṇas* manifest, *jīva* and *jagat* as *aṃśa* through *avirbhāva-tirobhāva*, the rejection of *māyā-vāda* — and is also a major work of devotional exegesis, especially on the tenth book, where Vraja-līlā is read as the central self-disclosure of Bhagavān and the model for *Puṣṭi-Mārga* *seva*. The *Subodhinī* is the principal Puṣṭimārga reference for *Bhāgavata*-interpretation and the spiritual heart of the lineage's textual practice.

engaged_works.title: Shodasha-Granthah
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sixteen short treatises foundational for the Puṣṭimārga.
engaged_works.summary: The *Ṣoḍaśa-Granthāḥ* are sixteen short Sanskrit tracts that together codify *Puṣṭi-Mārga* devotional practice and self-understanding. Among the most consequential are the *Yamunāṣṭakam* (eight verses on the river Yamunā as Kṛṣṇa-presence), *Bāla-Bodha* (an introductory doctrinal primer), *Siddhānta-Muktāvalī* (a verse summary of *śuddhādvaita*), *Puṣṭi-Pravāha-Maryādā* (on the typology of souls and the operation of *anugraha*), *Siddhānta-Rahasya* (the doctrinal foundation of the *brahma-saṃbandha* initiation that joins the *bhakta* to Kṛṣṇa), *Navaratnam* ("nine jewels" — a brief consolatory teaching on dependence on Kṛṣṇa), and the *Antaḥkaraṇa-Prabodha* and *Sarva-Nirṇaya* tracts on inner discernment. As a corpus the *Ṣoḍaśa-Granthāḥ* function as the lineage's portable devotional and doctrinal manual: short enough to be memorised, doctrinally precise, and addressed directly to the practitioner of *Puṣṭi-Mārga seva*. They are recited and studied in Puṣṭimārga households to this day.
